comments:
Summary and strengths:
- The paper addresses the adversarial utility-based dueling bandits setting and proposes REX3, an EXP3-style algorithm adapted to relative feedback. The main theoretical contribution is a finite-time expected regret upper bound O(√(K ln K T)) and a matching lower bound Ω(√(K T)) (via reduction). The algorithm centralizes information into a single weight vector (unlike SPARRING) and updates the two played arms in opposite directions. Empirical evaluation on several IR-derived preference matrices is reported and shows competitive behavior, especially at short horizons or large K.

Major positives:
- The core idea (extending exponential-weight methods to relative feedback) is natural and meaningful; the algorithm is simple and computationally attractive (maintains K weights rather than O(K^2) actions). 
- The upper-bound analysis adapts EXP3-style arguments to the relative-feedback estimator; the main lemmas and proof sketches are provided and seem plausible. 
- The experimental evaluation uses real preference matrices from IR and compares against state-of-the-art baselines including SPARRING, RUCB, BTM and SAVAGE.

Concerns and suggestions (things to improve / clarify):
1) Assumptions and scope. The algorithm and most of the analysis focus on the utility-based formulation (rewards for each arm, comparisons induced by utilities) and, in analysis, on ψ being the identity. The paper should clearly state the limitations: the results do not cover arbitrary matrix-based dueling bandits (no inherent utilities) and behavior under other choices of ψ (e.g. binary win/tie/loss) should be discussed more explicitly. If possible, include statements about how results adapt for ψ=Jx>0K.

2) Lower bound argument. The lower bound is obtained by reduction to the classical stochastic MAB, hence the construction uses a stationary stochastic distribution. The paper should clarify whether the Ω(√(K T)) lower bound applies to the fully adversarial setting or only rules out o(√(K T)) in general (including stochastic cases). Make explicit what class of environments the lower bound covers.

3) Proof detail and readability. Several steps are given as sketches or deferred to appendices; some derivations (e.g. bounds on M2, handling of negative ĉi values) could use clearer exposition. A full, self-contained proof (or clearer pointers to appendices with numbered steps) would help readers verify correctness more easily.

4) Experimental details. The experiments are promising but some important details are missing or insufficiently highlighted: precise hyperparameter settings for baselines and REX3 (how γ was chosen in practice), variance/error bars on plots, run-time/per-round complexity comparisons, and whether the stochastic-setting baselines were given any horizon information. Also it would strengthen claims to include explicit adversarial/non-stationary experiments (the paper mentions a non-stationary simulation in extended material — bring key results into the main text) to demonstrate the algorithm's advantage in adversarial regimes.

5) Any-time behavior and doubling trick. The anytime variant recomputes γ adaptively; the paper should describe the update schedule in full (or reference an established scheme) and report its empirical sensitivity.

6) Presentation issues. The submitted text contains duplicated paragraphs and some typographical errors that make parts harder to read (this looks like formatting problems in the provided file). Cleaning these up will improve accessibility.

Minor points and suggestions:
- Discuss the practical effect of sampling with replacement (self-duels) and frequency of no-information rounds when p concentrates; any heuristics to avoid wasting draws could be discussed. 
- Report statistical significance for empirical comparisons and include comparisons to Sparring+UCB in more settings (if not already in extended material). 
- Consider releasing code to aid reproducibility.

Overall assessment:
The paper makes a solid contribution: a natural and well-motivated extension of EXP3 to dueling bandits with matching upper and lower bounds and useful empirical validation. The main theoretical claims appear sound though some proofs and assumptions should be made clearer. Experiments would benefit from more detail and adversarial scenarios. I believe the work is of interest to the learning-theory and bandit communities and warrants dissemination after addressing the clarifications above.
